title: A very special traffic cone arrives in Boston, courtesy of our new friends in Glasgow
author: Heather Alterisio
bullets: A special traffic cone signed by Glasgow Chamber of Commerce's Stuart Patrick arrived in Boston as part of city partnership.
Massachusetts officials will showcase the cone at local venues to fundraise for mental health resources in both regions.
Boston and Glasgow are formalizing a sister city partnership, building cultural and economic ties established during the 2026 FIFA World Cup.

title: 3 more MBTA employees charged with overtime fraud, falsifying track inspections
author: Abby Patkin
bullets: Three more MBTA employees charged with falsifying Red Line track inspections and committing overtime fraud.
New conspiracy charges filed against four former Cabot Yard employees in ongoing MBTA investigation.
Seven MBTA employees, including supervisor Brian Pfaffinger, pleaded not guilty to federal charges.

title: The World Cup was great, but Boston still doesn’t need the Olympics
author: Hayden Bird
bullets: The World Cup concluded in Boston with France winning the last of seven matches, held at Gillette Stadium.
Boston's involvement in the World Cup was positively received, ranking top among U.S. venues by SeatPick.
Boston previously considered an Olympic bid requiring $4.7 billion, facing criticism for underestimating potential costs.

title: Bill Belichick’s daughter-in-law wanted for arrest in North Carolina after traffic stop
author: Khari A. Thompson
bullets: Jen Belichick, Bill Belichick's daughter-in-law, faces arrest in North Carolina after missing a court appearance for speeding.
Jen Belichick was cited for driving 88 mph in a 70 mph zone in Brunswick County, North Carolina.
Bill Belichick, now head coach at UNC, signed a $50 million deal in 2024, making him North Carolina's highest-paid public employee.

title: Karen Read, Turtleboy look to get witnesses’ defamation suit tossed
author: Abby Patkin
bullets: Karen Read and blogger Aidan Kearney filed to dismiss a defamation lawsuit from witnesses in Read's murder case.
Witnesses allege a defamation campaign by Read and Kearney related to the 2022 death of Boston Police Officer John O'Keefe.
Read and Kearney argue their actions are protected by Massachusetts' anti-SLAPP law, citing public involvement in the case.

title: The invisible force helping fuel this summer’s intense heat, and all our weather
author: David Epstein
bullets: The jet stream is linked to weather extremes, causing heatwaves from France to Boston this summer.
Quasi-resonant amplification can trap high pressure systems, intensifying heat waves and drought conditions.
Scientists struggle to model how the jet stream's movements cause regional weather extremes and heat domes.

title: Patriots 2026 position preview: Offensive line edition
author: Khari A. Thompson
bullets: The New England Patriots' offensive line showed improvement in 2025 but remains a concern for the 2026 season.
Key players in 2026 include Will Campbell, Alijah Vera-Tucker, Jared Wilson, and veteran Morgan Moses mentoring younger tackles.
Patriots' offensive line ranked sixth in pass-block efficiency in 2025, despite challenges in postseason performance.

title: New mural brings fresh life to the corner of Doyle’s and the Midway Cafe
author: Beth Treffeisen
bullets: A mural in Jamaica Plain commemorates Anthony Bourdain and Barack Obama's Hanoi visit from a decade ago.
The mural aims to revitalize the corner between the former Doyle's Cafe and Midway Cafe, closed since 2019.
Artist Lee Beard, known as SOÉMS, created the mural commissioned by developer Lee Goodman.

title: Largest known bottlenose dolphin stranding hits Cape Cod
author: Beth Treffeisen
bullets: As many as 30 bottlenose dolphins stranded off Brewster, Cape Cod on Monday during low tide; several died.
Rescuers found 19 dolphins in Wellfleet on Tuesday, fitted two with satellite tags; all refloated at high tide.
IFAW monitors the situation, warns more strandings possible; public advised to contact rescuers for stranded dolphins.

title: Personal ties are enhancing the tall ships experience at Sail Boston
author: Laurie Fullerton
bullets: Sail Boston 2026 features nearly 60 visiting tall ships, with 12 offering charter sails and many available for tours.
The Bluenose II from Nova Scotia is popular due to its historical ties with Boston and local settlement connections.
Spirit of Bermuda and Malabar X highlight the event with historical links and sailing experiences reminiscent of past maritime history.
